perturbation.original_text: 1.3.2 "Restricted Activities" means the operation of (i) a wholesale merchandise business that supplies stores of the type described in clause (ii) hereof, or (ii) any store engaged in the retail sale of goods (e.g., toys, health and beauty aids, food, books, party goods, stationery, hardware, housewares, jewelry, hair products, crafts, pet supplies, etc.) where at least eighty percent (80%) of such goods are sold at or below $2.00.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.3.2 "Restricted Activities" means the operation of (i) a wholesale merchandise business that supplies stores of the type described in clause (ii) hereof, or (ii) any store engaged in the retail sale of goods where more than fifty percent (50%) of such goods are sold at any price.
perturbation.explanation: The original text defines restricted activities based on selling goods at or below a specific price ($2.00). Modifying it to include stores where 'more than fifty percent (50%) of such goods are sold at any price' broadens the definition significantly. This creates ambiguity as the definition then includes any retail store, contradicting the intent of a non-compete agreement focused on a specific business model. It could include businesses that are not really competitors.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 16600 generally invalidates contracts that restrain anyone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind. Overly broad definitions of 'Restricted Activities,' like the modified definition, could be interpreted as an unlawful restraint of trade because it is so broad and includes businesses that are not actually competitors.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)

(b)(1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. perturbation.justification: The modified text broadens the definition of 'Restricted Activities' to include any store where 'more than fifty percent (50%) of such goods are sold at any price.' This contradicts California Business and Professions Code § 16600, which states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." As per the law explanation, overly broad definitions of 'Restricted Activities,' like the modified one, could be interpreted as an unlawful restraint of trade because they encompass businesses that are not actual competitors. The original text targeted a specific type of low-cost retailer. The modified text could include almost any retailer, unduly restricting competition, which BPC 16600 aims to prevent. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this by stating that the law 'shall be read broadly...to void the application of any noncompete agreement...no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter.'

perturbation.original_text: 1.1.2 operate or promote within the Restricted Area a store using (or at least a 500 square foot area within a store dedicated to) a single price point retail concept selling goods at a single price point at or below $2.00; or
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1.2 operate or promote within the Restricted Area a store, regardless of its size or pricing model; or
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the specific limitation to a 'single price point retail concept' and 'selling goods at a single price point at or below $2.00.' By removing these constraints, the non-compete clause now prohibits operating *any* store within the restricted area. This creates a legally questionable broad restriction, potentially preventing the Selling Shareholder from engaging in any retail business, not just competing single-price-point businesses.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 16600 prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. This modification potentially violates this law by creating an overly broad restriction that prevents the Selling Shareholder from operating *any* store in the restricted area.

perturbation.justification: The modification broadens the non-compete clause by removing specific limitations, potentially violating California Business and Professions Code § 16600. The original text restricted operating a store with a single price point below $2.00. The changed text prohibits operating any store, regardless of size or pricing, in the restricted area. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The modified contract creates an overly broad restriction, potentially preventing the Selling Shareholder from operating any retail business, not just competing single-price-point businesses, thus contradicting the law.

perturbation.original_text: As used herein, the term "affiliate" with respect to any person shall mean any person or entity which controls such person, which that person controls, or which is under common control with that person. In the case of Selling Shareholder, the term "affiliate" shall include the spouse, sibling, or lineal descendant or ancestor of Selling Shareholder, a trust for the benefit of any of the preceding, or any corporation, partnership, joint venture, or other entity which the Selling Shareholder, the spouse, sibling, or lineal descendant or ancestor of Selling Shareholder, or a trust for the benefit of any of the preceding, controls.
perturbation.changed_text: As used herein, the term "affiliate" with respect to any person shall mean any person or entity which has any business dealings with such person. In the case of Selling Shareholder, the term "affiliate" shall include any person who has ever known the Selling Shareholder, regardless of relationship or connection.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'affiliate' is based on control and specific familial relationships. The modified definition broadens this term to include 'any person who has ever known the Selling Shareholder,' which is extremely broad and impractical. This expanded definition could unintentionally and unreasonably restrict the Selling Shareholder's activities by making almost anyone an 'affiliate' subject to the non-compete clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification could violate California Business and Professions Code § 16600 by making the non-compete agreement unreasonably broad and restrictive. By extending the definition of 'affiliate' to anyone who has ever known the Selling Shareholder, the agreement could effectively prevent a large number of people from engaging in lawful business, which the original agreement intended to target.

perturbation.justification: The modified definition of 'affiliate' expands the term to 'any person who has ever known the Selling Shareholder.' This directly contradicts California Business and Professions Code § 16600, which voids contracts that restrain anyone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. By broadly defining 'affiliate,' the modified agreement could prevent a large number of people from engaging in lawful business, effectively creating an overly broad non-compete, as stated in the Law Explanation. The scraped snippets from California Code, BPC 16600, confirm that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' The modified definition, therefore, introduces a legal contradiction by potentially restraining a vast number of individuals, violating the legal intent to prevent overly broad restrictions on trade.

perturbation.original_text: The Selling Shareholder acknowledges that Dollar Tree shall have no adequate remedy at law and that irreparable injury may result to Dollar Tree in the event Selling Shareholder violates or breaches any of the above agreements, covenants, or restrictions. Therefore, it is hereby agreed that upon any breach of any of the above covenants, agreements, or restrictions, Dollar Tree shall be entitled, in addition to any other remedies available, to an injunction to restrain the violation or breach of any of the terms of such covenants, agreements, or restrictions by Selling Shareholder, and that no bond shall be required in procuring any such injunction.
perturbation.changed_text: The Selling Shareholder acknowledges that Dollar Tree shall have no adequate remedy at law and that any injury, whether direct or indirect, may result to Dollar Tree in the event Selling Shareholder violates or breaches any of the above agreements, covenants, or restrictions. Therefore, it is hereby agreed that upon any alleged breach of any of the above covenants, agreements, or restrictions, Dollar Tree shall be entitled, in addition to any other remedies available, to an injunction to restrain the alleged violation or breach of any of the terms of such covenants, agreements, or restrictions by Selling Shareholder, and that no bond shall be required in procuring any such injunction.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that Dollar Tree can seek an injunction upon a breach by the Selling Shareholder. The modified text changes 'any breach' to 'any alleged breach'. By using the word 'alleged', the contract suggests that Dollar Tree can obtain an injunction even without proof of an actual breach, potentially lowering the threshold for injunctive relief.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 65 governs the issuance of preliminary injunctions and requires a showing of likelihood of success on the merits. By allowing for injunctions based on 'alleged' breaches without requiring substantial proof, the modified contract conflicts with the standards set by FRCP 65 for obtaining injunctive relief, making it more difficult for a court to uphold the provision.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders (a) Preliminary Injunction. (1) Notice. The court may issue a preliminary injunction only on notice to the adverse party. (2) Consolidating the Hearing with the Trial on the Merits. Before or after beginning the hearing on a motion for a preliminary injunction, the court may advance the trial on the merits and consolidate it with the hearing. Even when consolidation is not ordered, evidence that is received on the motion and that would be admissible at trial becomes part of the trial record and need not be repeated at trial. But the court must preserve any party's right to a jury trial. (b) Temporary Restraining Order. (1) Issuing Without Notice. The court may issue a temporary restraining order without written or oral notice to the adverse party or its attorney only if: (A) specific facts in an affidavit or a verified complaint clearly show that immediate and irreparable injury, loss, or damage will result to the movant before the adverse party can be heard in opposition; and (B) the movant's attorney certifies in writing any efforts made to give notice and the reasons why it should not be required. (2) Contents; Expiration. Every temporary restraining order issued without notice must state the date and hour it was issued; describe the injury and state why it is irreparable; state why the order was issued without notice; and be promptly filed in the clerk's office and entered in the record. The order expires at the time after entry—not to exceed 14 days—that the court sets, unless before that time the court, for good cause, extends it for a like period or the adverse party consents to a longer extension. The reasons for an extension must be entered in the record. (3) Expediting the Preliminary-Injunction Hearing. If the order is issued without notice, the moti
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces "any breach" with "any alleged breach," which lowers the threshold for Dollar Tree to seek an injunction. This contradicts the standard for preliminary injunctions, which typically requires demonstrating a likelihood of success on the merits (as described in the Law Explanation, which refers to FRCP 65). While the provided snippets do not explicitly state the 'likelihood of success' standard, snippet 1, referencing Rule 65, focuses on the conditions and procedures for issuing injunctions, indicating that an injunction is not a simple matter of 'alleged' breach but a process governed by rules. The change undermines the due process and legal requirements implicitly acknowledged in Rule 65 for obtaining injunctive relief, suggesting a clear non-compliance because it attempts to circumvent the established legal procedure before a breach is even proven.

perturbation.original_text: Selling Shareholder has been encouraged by Dollar Tree to have this Agreement reviewed by Selling Shareholder's independent legal counsel and has had adequate opportunity to do so. Selling Shareholder agrees that the restrictions, covenants and agreements contained in this Agreement are (i) reasonable in all respects (including, without limitation, geographic area and duration), (ii) necessary to protect the value of Dollar Tree, and (iii) are not unduly harsh upon Selling Shareholder's ability to earn a livelihood.
perturbation.changed_text: Selling Shareholder acknowledges having the option to consult legal counsel and asserts they are sufficiently sophisticated to understand the terms. Selling Shareholder agrees that the restrictions, covenants and agreements contained in this Agreement are (i) fair, irrespective of geographic area and duration, (ii) beneficial to Dollar Tree, and (iii) inconsequential to Selling Shareholder's ability to earn a livelihood.
perturbation.explanation: The original text emphasizes reasonableness and lack of undue harshness on the Selling Shareholder. The modified text changes 'reasonable' to 'fair, irrespective of geographic area and duration,' 'necessary' to 'beneficial', and 'not unduly harsh' to 'inconsequential.' These changes weaken the protections for the Selling Shareholder by downplaying the importance of the geographic scope and duration of the non-compete, suggesting the terms are merely 'beneficial' rather than 'necessary', and dismissing the potential impact on the Selling Shareholder's livelihood as 'inconsequential'.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 16600 disfavors restraints of trade. By altering the language to diminish the importance of reasonableness and hardship on the employee, the modified text makes the agreement potentially less likely to be upheld under California law, as it suggests a disregard for the employee's rights and the potential restraint on their ability to earn a livelihood.

perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens protections for the Selling Shareholder, potentially contravening California's stance against restraint of trade as described in BPC § 16600. The original text focuses on 'reasonableness' and preventing 'undue harshness,' whereas the changed text uses terms like 'fair' and 'inconsequential,' which suggest a lower standard of protection. BPC § 16600(a) states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' By diminishing the emphasis on reasonableness and impact on the employee's livelihood, the changes in the modified text make the agreement less likely to be upheld under this statute, as it may be viewed as an unreasonable restraint of trade. The scraped snippets reinforce the broad interpretation against non-compete agreements, thus the change creates a notable contradiction.
